payload.action: Maintain clear positioning: damask is a knowledge graph, not a vector store. Borrow search UX ideas, not architecture.
payload.summary: OneContext comparison: damask is NOT a RAG system and should not become one. OneContext solves document-to-vector-index retrieval. Damask solves structured-annotation-over-code graph queries. The intersection is search UX — both retrieve context for agents, but damask retrieves typed relationships while OneContext retrieves semantic chunks.

payload.action: Design a hybrid_search mode: FTS5 for keywords + optional embedding similarity for payloads. Could delegate to ck-search MCP or embed locally.
payload.summary: OneContext lesson: hybrid search (semantic + keyword + metadata filters) dramatically outperforms keyword-only FTS. damask search currently uses FTS5 alone. Adding optional semantic similarity (even via a lightweight local embedding model or external MCP tool) for edge payload search would close a real gap.

payload.action: Add [ranking] section to damask.toml with configurable signal weights. Let users tune without code changes.
payload.summary: OneContext lesson: YAML-declarative pipelines separate concern of what-to-retrieve from how-to-use-it. damask has no pipeline concept by design, but the ranking system (10 signals) is effectively a scoring pipeline. Exposing ranking config as a declarative file (damask.toml weights) would give agents the same iterate-on-retrieval-quality loop.

payload.action: Build damask-mcp crate exposing orient, search, at, edge as MCP tools. Priority: this is the highest-leverage affordance gap.
payload.summary: OneContext lesson: they expose exactly ONE MCP tool (query_knowledge_base) — dead simple agent integration. damask has 12+ CLI commands but no MCP server. Wrapping damask as an MCP server with 3-4 tools (query, record, endorse, orient) would make it instantly usable by any MCP-aware agent without shell access.

payload.action: Add --where predicate flag to damask search, combine FTS5 match with predicate filter in one SQL query.
payload.summary: OneContext lesson: metadata filtering on queries (author=X, category=Y) is first-class. damask where supports predicates but damask search does not combine FTS with predicate filters in one query. Agents need to pipe search | where manually. Adding --where to search would match the OneContext UX.

payload.action: Design damask eval: curated test queries with expected edge IDs, compute precision@k and recall@k. Ship as damask test-set command.
payload.summary: OneContext lesson: evaluation/test sets for retrieval quality (precision, recall, truthfulness). damask lint checks annotation quality but has no way to evaluate retrieval quality — no ground-truth test sets, no precision@k metrics. For agent workflows, knowing whether damask search returns the right edges matters.

payload.action: Wire ranking.rs into search and where commands as a --ranked flag. Score edges after retrieval, re-sort by composite score.
payload.summary: OneContext lesson: reranking as a distinct retrieval stage. damask has a 10-signal ranking system in ranking.rs but it is not wired into search or where output. FTS5 rank is used raw. Applying the composite ranking as a reranking pass on search results would dramatically improve result quality.

Wrong product analyzed. OneContext (TheAgentContextLab) is an agent session/context management layer, NOT the onecontext.ai RAG pipeline company. Completely different product category — much closer to damask domain.

payload.action: Re-evaluate all lessons through this lens — OneContext solves agent continuity, not document retrieval.
payload.summary: CORRECTED: OneContext (TheAgentContextLab) is an agent self-managed context layer — records agent trajectories across sessions, enables sharing/loading context so any agent can continue from same point. Core concepts: Contexts (named knowledge containers), Sessions (work units with working paths under a context), auto-generated summaries, shareable links, archive/resume. This is a direct competitor to damask in the agent memory space.

payload.action: Add damask sessions command: list sessions with activity timeline and auto-summary. Add damask session <id> to show all edges from that session.
payload.summary: OneContext lesson 1 — SESSION AS FIRST-CLASS CONCEPT: OneContext groups agent work into sessions under a context. Damask has agent+session fields on spans/edges but they are optional metadata, not a queryable axis. damask where agent=X or session=Y works but there is no orient-by-session, no session timeline, no session summary. Adding a session-aware view (damask sessions, damask session <id>) would give agents the same continuity affordance.

payload.action: Design a trajectory recording mode: damask session start / damask session end that bookmarks agent activity. Or simpler: a rel=trajectory edge type with ordered payload.
payload.summary: OneContext lesson 2 — TRAJECTORY RECORDING (agent warm-start): OneContext auto-records what agents do and builds a trajectory that any future agent can load to continue from the same point. Damask records what agents FIND, not what they DO. The gap: damask has no way to say start-session/end-session or record the sequence of commands an agent ran. Adding trajectory edges (rel=trajectory, payload with tool calls and decisions) would close this — agents could replay or summarize prior work sessions.

payload.action: Add damask orient --export that produces a portable markdown briefing of current state. Consider a damask share command that generates a gist or uploads to a paste service.
payload.summary: OneContext lesson 3 — SHAREABLE CONTEXT LINKS: OneContext generates a link + message that anyone can use to load context, even in Slack. Damask contexts are git-checked-in JSONL — shareable via git but not via a URL. The gap is not the storage (JSONL-in-git is stronger than a link) but the onboarding UX. A damask orient --share that produces a self-contained markdown briefing (or a hosted link) would give the same handoff affordance.

payload.action: Add damask orient --summarize that uses an LLM to synthesize top risks, decisions, and recent activity into a narrative paragraph. Store result as a describes edge for caching.
payload.summary: OneContext lesson 4 — AUTO-GENERATED SUMMARIES: Sessions get auto-summaries shown in the sidebar; contexts get short + detailed summaries on hover. Damask orient is the closest equivalent but it is manual and shows raw edges. The gap: no LLM-generated synthesis. Adding an optional --summarize flag to orient that feeds top edges to an LLM for a narrative summary would match this. Could also auto-generate namespace summaries at compact time.

payload.action: Design damask bootstrap: scan repo structure, README, CI, key source files. Use LLM to generate initial edges. This is higher leverage than OneContext sharing for solo/greenfield use.
payload.summary: OneContext lesson 5 — COLD START SOLVED BY LOADING SHARED CONTEXT: OneContext addresses cold start by letting you import a shared session. Damask addresses cold start via orient (read the graph). But orient on a cold graph is useless — cold_start=true means no edges to read. The real gap: damask has no bootstrap command. A damask bootstrap that auto-scans the repo (README, CI config, key files) and creates initial describes/decision/risk edges from LLM analysis would solve cold start. OneContext solves it via human sharing; damask could solve it via automated analysis.

payload.action: Add damask ingest-git: scan recent commits, auto-create co_change edges for files that changed together, describes edges for new files. Run as post-commit hook or CI step.
payload.summary: OneContext lesson 6 — CONTEXT GROWS OVER TIME BY DEFAULT: OneContext passively records agent work. In damask, context only grows when agents explicitly call damask edge. The skill prompt teaches agents to do this, but without the skill, damask is silent. The gap: damask could add a post-commit hook or CI step that auto-creates edges from git diffs (co_change, describes for new files). This would make the graph grow passively like OneContext.

MCP deprioritized to P3. Feb 2026 consensus: CLI tools beat MCP servers for developer tools — zero config, full composability, no token bloat, no server lifecycle. Damask CLI already works with every shell-capable agent. MCP only matters for agents without shell access (minority for dev tools). Protocol still unstable (transport changes, 43% of servers have injection vulns per Equixly).

snippet: //! Core data types for the Damask knowledge fabric.

payload.action: Reframe damask from knowledge-fabric-for-code to institutional-memory-for-agent-teams. Deprioritize retrieval features. Double down on temporal/social/cross-session features.
payload.summary: Bitter lesson risk: ~50% of damask features (search, retrieval, indexing, resolution cascade, ranking) are workarounds for limited context windows. Opus 4.6 scores 76% on MRCR v2 at 1M tokens (vs 18.5% previous gen). Context windows growing 5x per model generation. Retrieval/indexing features will be obsoleted by brute-force context scaling within 1-2 generations. The durable half is institutional memory: cross-session state, decisions, endorsement/dispute, decay, audit trails — things not derivable from reading code.

Reframing: damask is not memory (replaceable by context scaling). It is structured annotation — typed, scored, code-pinned observations that become part of the codebase artifact, like types or tests. Types are not obsoleted by bigger context windows. Neither are annotations. Bigger windows actually make annotations more useful by letting models reason over code + annotations together. The bitter lesson does not apply to annotation layers.
